front: What is the core principle of the Breadth-First Search (BFS) algorithm?
back: BFS explores a graph or tree level by level. It visits all the neighbors (children) of the current node before moving on to nodes at the next depth level. It uses a First-In-First-Out (FIFO) queue to manage the order of exploration.

front: What data structure does BFS use to keep track of nodes to be explored, and why?
back: BFS uses a queue (a FIFO - First-In-First-Out data structure). This ensures that nodes are explored in the exact order they are discovered, which is essential for visiting all nodes at the present depth prior to moving on to nodes at the next depth level.

front: What are the key properties of BFS regarding completeness, optimality, and time/space complexity?
back: Completeness: Yes, if the branching factor (b) is finite. Optimality: Yes, for unit step cost (finds the shallowest solution). Time Complexity: O(b^d). Space Complexity: O(b^d), where b is the branching factor and d is the depth of the shallowest solution. It is space-intensive.

front: In the context of BFS, what does it mean for the algorithm to be 'complete'?
back: A complete algorithm is guaranteed to find a solution if one exists. BFS is complete because it will eventually explore every node in a finite graph or all nodes up to the depth of a solution in an infinite graph with a finite branching factor.

front: What is the main disadvantage of BFS compared to Depth-First Search (DFS)?
back: The main disadvantage is its high memory consumption (space complexity). Because it stores all nodes at the current depth level and their children in the queue, its space requirement of O(b^d) grows exponentially with the depth of the search, making it impractical for problems with large solution depths.
